description: The GPIO module provides up to 32 signals grouped into two banks of 16 (GP[31:0]). Control registers are organized as 32-bit registers per pair of banks, with bit positions mapping to individual GPIOs. This banked structure standardizes access and scales predictably across pins. It enables per-pin control while allowing per-bank interrupt enables. Use Table 1 to map signal numbers to register bits.
Remarks: Plan software to iterate over banks for bulk configuration and status collection.

description: GPIO pins share package balls via device-wide pin multiplexing. Multiplexing is configured by a combination of reset configuration and software-programmable registers outside the GPIO module. Proper pinmux is a prerequisite before GPIO control and interrupt use. The actual pin assignment and mux options are device-specific. Refer to the device data manual for exact mux settings.
dependencies: Device pinmux registers (device data manual); Reset configuration
Remarks: Validate mux state before enabling GPIO direction or interrupts to avoid bus contention.

description: GPIO operation is independent of endianness. No byte ordering considerations apply when accessing GPIO registers. This simplifies driver design across big- and little-endian CPU modes. The same register programming model applies in all cases. No special alignment handling is required for this peripheral.
Remarks: Use standard 32-bit memory-mapped accesses regardless of CPU endianness.

description: Each GPIO pin is configurable as input (1) or output (0) using the DIR01 register. By default, all GPIO pins come up as inputs after reset. Direction can be changed per pin without affecting others. Correct direction setting is required for output drive or input sampling to be effective. Use Table 1 to locate the DIR bit for a given pin.
Remarks: Set direction before writing outputs or enabling edge interrupts to ensure intended behavior.

description: Output state can be driven via three registers: OUT_DATA01 for direct read-modify-write, SET_DATA01 to atomically set bits high, and CLR_DATA01 to atomically clear bits low. SET/CLR allow multiple software processes to manipulate outputs without critical section protection. Reads of OUT/SET/CLR reflect register values, not actual pin state if configured as input. Use IN_DATA01 to observe external signal levels. This allows bulk programming of output states when concurrency is controlled.
dependencies: DIR configured as output; PSC clock enabled
Remarks: Prefer SET/CLR for concurrency-safe toggling; reserve OUT for bulk updates when interrupts are masked.

description: The IN_DATA01 register returns the current logic level per pin synchronized to the GPIO clock. For input-configured pins, it reflects the external signal state after synchronization. For output-configured pins, it returns the device-driven value (or wired-logic value for open-drain cases noted in the data manual). Sampling is per pin and non-destructive. Use this to poll pins or confirm output state.
Remarks: Account for clock-domain synchronization latency when timing edge-sensitive reads.

description: Every GPIO pin can generate interrupts on rising, falling, or both edges via SET_RIS_TRIG01/CLR_RIS_TRIG01 and SET_FAL_TRIG01/CLR_FAL_TRIG01. Edge detection is synchronized to the GPIO module clock. Interrupt generation does not require the pin to be configured as input, enabling software-driven test interrupts. Configuration is independent per pin. Disable edges explicitly to prevent spurious events.
dependencies: Interrupt controller routing (device-specific); BINTEN per-bank enable; PSC clock enabled
Remarks: Program only required edges and clear any pending status before enabling CPU-level routing.

description: GPIO interrupt events are enabled in groups of 16 pins using BINTEN bits EN0 (bank0) and EN1 (bank1). These bits control both CPU interrupt requests and EDMA events for the bank. Disabling a bank masks all per-pin interrupt outputs from that bank. Bank enable works in conjunction with per-pin edge settings. Ensure device-specific interrupt numbers are mapped appropriately.
dependencies: Per-pin edge configuration; CPU interrupt controller; EDMA event mapping
Remarks: Enable banks only after per-pin edge configuration to avoid unintended first interrupts.

description: Pending GPIO interrupts are indicated in INTSTAT01, one bit per pin. Bits are cleared by writing a 1 (write-one-to-clear), while writing 0 has no effect. Status can be used to disambiguate which pin triggered a bank interrupt. Software is responsible for servicing and clearing all pending sources. Use before exiting the ISR to avoid lost events.
Remarks: Always read-modify-write with care: write back only the bits you intend to clear to preserve others.

description: GPIO can generate synchronization events to the EDMA in addition to CPU interrupts. This enables DMA-triggered workflows on GPIO edges without CPU polling. Event routing details (event numbers) are device-specific and defined in the data manual. Bank enable (BINTEN) gates these events. Use for low-latency, low-overhead edge capture.
dependencies: EDMA; BINTEN; Device event crossbar (device data manual)
Remarks: Confirm EDMA event mapping and priority to avoid conflicts with other peripherals.

description: The GPIO input clock is disabled by default and must be enabled through the local Power and Sleep Controller (LPSC/PSC). The GPIO module clock runs at CPU/6 once enabled. Without clock enable, register access and interrupt logic are inactive. Proper power/clock sequencing is required after reset. Clock changes impact edge synchronization timing.
Remarks: Enable the PSC module before any GPIO configuration and consider clock ratios when budgeting interrupt latency.

description: The PSC can place the GPIO peripheral into reduced-power states to save energy. While in low-power state, interrupt generation is suspended, but output-configured pins maintain their last driven state. Exiting low power restores normal operation after re-enabling the clock. Power transitions do not alter output latch contents. Coordinate with system sleep states.
Remarks: Quiesce GPIO interrupts before entering low power and reinitialize any timing-sensitive logic on wake.

description: A software reset via emulator does not modify GPIO configuration or signal states. A PSC reset (clock disable, PSC reset, re-enable) returns configuration registers to defaults. A hardware reset resets GPIO configuration and data registers to default states, affecting signal configuration. Understanding reset sources is critical for deterministic boot. Plan reinitialization accordingly.
Remarks: After hardware reset perform full GPIO init; after emulator reset verify state before assuming defaults.

description: After hardware reset, initialize GPIO by: configuring pin multiplexing, enabling the module via PSC, and programming direction/data/interrupt control registers. This order ensures pins are safely muxed before being driven. Enabling the clock precedes any register access. Finalize by verifying IN/OUT states and clearing pending interrupts. The module is then ready for data transactions.
Remarks: Follow the documented order to avoid unintended drive on shared pins during bring-up.

description: The GPIO peripheral is not affected by emulation suspend events such as halts and breakpoints. GPIO outputs and interrupt logic continue according to hardware state. This has implications for debugging time-sensitive interactions. Use caution when halting the CPU while external systems depend on GPIO activity. Hardware continues autonomous behavior.
Remarks: Design debug procedures that avoid halting when GPIO-driven external devices require continuous signaling.

description: The PID register provides peripheral identification fields including scheme, function code (483h for GPIO), major/minor revision, and RTL/custom IDs. It is read-only and fixed by hardware. Software can use these fields to validate the peripheral instance and apply workarounds by revision. Accessing PID does not affect operation. It resides at offset 0x0 in the GPIO register map.
Remarks: Log PID on boot to aid field diagnostics and compatibility checks.

description: Separate SET_DATA01 and CLR_DATA01 provide atomic set/clear semantics to avoid critical sections. Multiple software agents can assert or deassert specific outputs without read-modify-write races. This reduces ISR latency and improves determinism. OUT_DATA01 remains available for bulk state programming. Choose the method that best matches concurrency requirements.
dependencies: DIR configured as output; Interrupt masking policy
Remarks: Use SET/CLR in multi-threaded or interrupt-driven contexts to prevent lost bit updates.

feature_name: Edge detection synchronized to GPIO clock
description: All interrupt edge detection is synchronized to the GPIO peripheral clock domain. This ensures metastability-safe sensing but introduces up to a cycle of detection latency. Sampling and event timing therefore depend on the CPU/6-derived GPIO clock. Designs should account for this when measuring narrow pulses. Polling via IN_DATA01 observes the synchronized state as well.
dependencies: PSC clock enabled; CPU clock configuration
Remarks: For very short pulses consider external conditioning or alternate capture peripherals if timing margins are tight.

description: Some signals may utilize open-drain output buffers for wired-logic operations as documented in the device data manual. For such pins, IN_DATA01 reflects the wired-logic level on the signal rather than the device's drive alone. This distinction matters when external pull-ups/pull-downs are present. The GPIO module itself does not configure open-drain behavior. Consult the device manual for pin-level electrical traits.
Remarks: When using open-drain pins, verify external biasing to ensure correct readback and rise/fall times.

feature_name: No interrupt multiplexing with other functions
description: GPIO interrupts are not multiplexed with other interrupt functions on this device. This simplifies routing and avoids shared-vector ambiguity. Software can assume dedicated sources for GPIO-related interrupts. Bank and per-pin configuration still determine which lines are active. Refer to the device data manual for vector numbers.
dependencies: CPU interrupt controller; Device vector map
Remarks: Use distinct ISRs for GPIO vectors and keep service routines minimal to reduce latency.

description: The GPIO module exposes a linear register map including PID, BINTEN, DIR01, OUT_DATA01, SET_DATA01, CLR_DATA01, IN_DATA01, edge trigger controls, and INTSTAT01. Offsets are listed in Table 2 and are memory-mapped via the device bus. Access width is 32 bits. Reserved address locations must not be written. The actual base address is device-specific.
dependencies: System memory map; Bus access permissions
Remarks: Abstract register addresses via a header to prevent hardcoding and ease porting across variants.

description: The memory-mapped base address for this GPIO controller instance is provided in the memory map. All register offsets are relative to this base. Correct base address is required for driver initialization and access. Using an incorrect base leads to bus faults or unintended peripheral access. Validate against platform memory map.
Remarks: Make base address configurable per board/SoC variant via build-time or device tree mechanisms.

description: The specification does not define GPIO-specific error status, fault injection, or protection registers. There are no explicit error reporting mechanisms beyond interrupt status for edge events. Bus errors on invalid access would be system-level, not GPIO-specific. Software should implement timeouts and sanity checks around register access. Any additional error signaling would be device-level and outside this module.
dependencies: System bus error handling; CPU exception handlers
Remarks: Implement defensive coding patterns (timeouts, assertions) since the GPIO IP provides no native error flags.

description: No security, access control, or privilege filtering mechanisms are documented for the GPIO module. Access is assumed to be via standard memory-mapped registers without hardware gating in this IP. Any security policy must be enforced by the SoC's system fabric or MPU/MMU. Do not assume pin state obfuscation or tamper detection. Review platform-level security architecture for protections.
Remarks: Restrict GPIO MMIO regions to trusted software contexts where required by the application.
